headline: What to Do Immediately After a Utah Work Injury: A Step-by-Step Guide
articleBody:
What to Do Immediately After a Utah Work Injury: A Step-by-Step Guide

Getting injured on the job is a disorienting and stressful experience. Whether it’s a sudden accident or a repetitive motion injury that has finally become unbearable, the steps you take in the first 72 hours are critical to ensuring your medical bills are paid and your lost wages are recovered.

1. Report the Injury Immediately

In Utah, you have a legal obligation to notify your employer within 30 days of the accident. However, waiting even a few days can give the insurance company an excuse to doubt your claim. They often argue that if you didn't report it immediately, the injury must have happened over the weekend or at home. Always report the injury in writing—text or email is better than a phone call because it creates a time-stamped paper trail.

2. Seek Medical Attention from an Authorized Provider

Tell the doctor exactly how the injury happened and emphasize that it occurred at work. This ensures the medical record is clear from day one. In many cases, your employer has the right to choose the doctor for your initial visit. Be sure to ask your supervisor if there is a 'preferred provider' you must see.

3. Be Specific About Your Symptoms

Don't be a 'tough guy.' If your back hurts but your knee also feels tingly, tell the doctor everything. If a body part isn't mentioned in the initial medical reports, it becomes much harder to get treatment for it later in the claim. The insurance company will look for any omission to deny care for secondary injuries.

4. File the Formal Claim

Reporting the injury to your boss is not the same as filing a claim with the state. Your employer should file an 'Employer’s First Report of Injury.' Ask for a copy of this. If they refuse or drag their feet, you may need to file a formal application for a hearing with the Utah Labor Commission yourself.

5. Document Everything

Keep a folder with every piece of paper you receive: doctor's notes, work status slips, pay stubs, and correspondence from the insurance company. Note down the dates of all conversations with your supervisor or the insurance adjuster.

When to Call a Lawyer

If your claim is denied, if you are being pressured to return to work before you are ready, or if your injuries are serious enough to require surgery, contacting an attorney immediately is the best way to protect your future. A lawyer can handle the paperwork and negotiations while you focus on healing.
